competencies.name: ML Fundamentals & Modeling
competencies.description: Understands core ML concepts; selects appropriate models for the problem; reasons about bias-variance tradeoff; understands regularization, overfitting, and generalization.
positive_indicators: selects model family with justification for the problem type
explains bias-variance tradeoff concretely
discusses regularization and when to apply it
mentions evaluation metrics appropriate to the task
identifies overfitting risk and mitigation strategies
negative_indicators: reaches for deep learning without considering simpler baselines
cannot explain why a model type was chosen
conflates training and validation error
cannot discuss regularization beyond naming it

competencies.name: Feature Engineering & Data Pipelines
competencies.description: Transforms raw data into meaningful features; handles missing values, categorical variables, and distribution issues; builds reliable, reproducible pipelines.
positive_indicators: explains feature encoding choices with justification
handles missing values thoughtfully rather than just dropping rows
discusses data leakage and how to prevent it
thinks about pipeline reproducibility and retraining
validates data assumptions before modeling
negative_indicators: cannot explain why a particular encoding was chosen
does not consider data leakage in the feature set
treats missing data carelessly
does not think about the pipeline beyond the notebook

competencies.name: Model Evaluation & Validation
competencies.description: Selects appropriate evaluation metrics; designs robust validation schemes; understands distribution shift; interprets model outputs critically.
positive_indicators: chooses metrics tied to business impact, not just accuracy
designs train/val/test splits to prevent leakage
discusses distribution shift and monitoring in production
interprets confusion matrix and threshold choices
mentions calibration for probability outputs
negative_indicators: defaults to accuracy without considering class imbalance
uses test set during model selection
does not mention monitoring after deployment
cannot interpret precision/recall tradeoff

competencies.name: Production ML Systems
competencies.description: Understands the challenges of deploying ML in production; thinks about latency, throughput, model drift, and monitoring; can describe a basic MLOps workflow.
positive_indicators: distinguishes between training and serving infrastructure
describes how they would monitor a model post-deployment
discusses model versioning and rollback strategy
considers latency constraints when choosing model complexity
mentions feature store or online/offline feature consistency
negative_indicators: treats deployment as handing off a pickle file
no mention of monitoring or drift detection
cannot reason about serving latency tradeoffs
has not thought about model versioning or A/B testing

competencies.name: Experimentation
competencies.description: Designs and interprets A/B tests; understands statistical significance and practical significance; knows when offline metrics translate (or don't) to online metrics.
positive_indicators: explains why randomization is necessary
discusses power calculation and sample size
distinguishes statistical significance from practical significance
identifies when offline improvements may not generalize online
mentions novelty effects and long-run metric stability
negative_indicators: cannot explain why A/B testing requires randomization
stops analysis at p < 0.05 without effect size discussion
assumes offline metrics always predict online performance
does not discuss test duration or sample size

competencies.name: Communication
competencies.description: Explains ML concepts to non-ML stakeholders; documents model decisions; communicates uncertainty and limitations clearly.
positive_indicators: explains a model's limitations to a non-technical stakeholder
quantifies uncertainty in outputs, not just point estimates
documents design decisions in code or model cards
negative_indicators: cannot explain model behavior to a non-technical audience
does not mention uncertainty or confidence intervals
has no practice of documenting model decisions
